allocations.rationale: Gold protects against inflation and currency swings. Over 7 years, it's expected to grow faster than bank deposits. Since you're in the New Regime, this counts as a 'safer option' and gains are taxed at your slab rate, which is fine given your long horizon.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Mid-sized companies have grown faster than big ones (17% on average). This index fund costs only 0.2% yearly fee (lower than actively managed funds). Over 7 years, this is time for growth, not caution. Since you're in the New Regime, gains after 1 year are taxed at 12.5% on profits over ₹1.25 lakh — very favourable.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Growth - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This is your 'safer anchor'. Locks in short-term government and bank bonds. Expected to give 10–12% over 7 years. Since you're shifting from FDs, this mimics FD safety but with higher returns and no lock-in. Gains are taxed at your slab rate.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This hybrid fund puts ~65% in stocks and ~35% in bonds — giving you growth plus some stability. It's equity-taxed (12.5% LTCG after 1 year), which beats slab-tax at your New Regime slab. Perfect for balancing risk.

fund.name: Invesco India Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Additional high-growth exposure. Flexi-cap funds can move between large and small companies. This fund has strong 3-year returns (20.9%). Use this to add a bit more growth punch since you're aggressive.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a Direct Mutual Fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or Coin using your first name (no paperwork needed). Step 2: Start SIPs in the 5 funds above. Step 3: After FDs mature, redeposit into the MF plan we discussed today. Step 4: Keep an emergency FD of ₹1–2 lakhs for 3 months’ expenses in traditional banking.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check that all 5 SIPs are running correctly. Fall behind? Don't worry — just let SIPs keep going.
After 1 year: Compare this portfolio to similar funds (check rankings on Value Research or Morningstar). Ensure no single fund has underperformed by >5%.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year (say, every March), check if your categories have drifted. If midcap has grown to 40% instead of 30%, sell some midcap and buy debt. Keep the mix steady. This is why you have a 7-year horizon — you can weather dips.
plan.rationale: You're 65, not retiring until 70, but already building wealth. Your portfolio must protect your existing 102L while growing enough for the remaining years. We've kept 40% in 'safer' debt funds because your income stability matters more now. Midcaps (30%) and flexibility (10%+10%) ensure you don't miss growth. The 10% gold cushion is your 'insurance'. In plain terms: you're betting on your time till 70 to compound, but with a safety net so a 20% drop doesn't panic you.
risks: Equity markets can drop 20–30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily fall. This is normal and recovers over time. The 40% debt cushion will cushion the blow.
Inflation (5–6% per year) will erode your purchasing power. That's why we put 45% in 'growth funds' (midcaps, flexi funds, gold).
Your New Regime slab (30% tax on debt, 12.5% LTCG on equity) means debt funds are post-tax at ~6–7%, while equity gives ~8–10% even after taxes over 7 years. The gap is wide.
plan.disclaimer: This plan is for educational/research purposes only. Not certified financial advice. Mutual funds are subject to market risks. Read all scheme-related documents carefully.

sip_step_up.description: Year 2: increase SIP by ₹8,700 (10%); Year 3: ₹17,400 more than Year 2, and so on. This matches expected salary revisions.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Start post-retirement, assuming FDs maturing at ₹30L
If equity drops below 35%, shift more to debt
As you approach 68, reduce risk further

pqs.reasoning: The plan scores poorly on risk appropriateness and goal alignment because it aggressively recommends a 30-40% allocation to risky Midcap and Flexible Cap funds for a 65-year-old with a 'wealth preservation' life stage and a goal to 'Retire comfortably with 5Cr monthly income'. A monthly income of 5Cr would require a corpus of roughly 250-300Cr (assuming 16-20% withdrawal rates), yet the investor has only 1Cr. The plan's rationale contradicts the life stage by emphasizing growth over preservation. Internally, the timeline for the annual review (checking rankings at 1 year) is too short for an aggressive midcap fund which is volatile. On tax efficiency, it scores well (0.7) as it correctly identifies that the 'New Regime' investor saves money by using Equity-taxed wrappers (Hybrid and Midcap) rather than debt funds, correctly noting that debt funds are taxed at the 30% marginal slab under the new regime. Diversification is good (0.8) as it covers Gold, Midcap, Debt, and Flexi-cap. Goal alignment is low (0.4) because the plan ignores the extreme ambition of the '5Cr income' goal and the preservation needs of age 65, instead pushing a high-risk strategy.
